skill.name: agentix-ceo
skill.description: Coordinate Agentix teams, roles, tasks, workers, playbooks, and credentials through the Agentix API.
skill.summary: Orchestrate Agentix worker teams from setup through task execution and monitoring.
skill.author: agentix-cloud

security_audit.summary: Static findings are mostly true positives: the skill directs agents to use external APIs, persist credentials in a hidden home directory file, handle API keys, and spawn remote workers. I did not find evidence of prompt injection or concealed malicious instructions, but the combination of credential collection, remote service calls, and worker execution is too privileged for publication without a strong trust review.

high_findings.title: Credential Collection and Remote Storage
high_findings.description: The skill instructs the agent to ask for Anthropic and GitHub credentials and submit them to the Agentix API. This is declared functionality, but it creates high impact exposure if the service, team configuration, or agent session is compromised.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The skill explicitly asks users for sensitive API keys and tokens, then sends them in API request bodies. This confirms credential handling and remote transmission, although the text states the purpose is worker configuration.

high_findings.title: Remote Worker Execution Through External Service
high_findings.description: The skill can create tasks and start or resume Agentix workers through API endpoints. Remote worker orchestration is core functionality, but it enables execution outside the user workspace and requires strong user trust.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file describes ephemeral workers and provides endpoints to spawn or resume them. The behavior is intentional, but the execution boundary and external control plane make the risk high.

high_findings.title: Persistent Hidden Credential File
high_findings.description: The skill directs agents to read and write credentials in ~/.agentix/credentials and to load values silently on session start. This supports convenience, but it stores high value secrets in a hidden home directory file.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file paths and credential fields are explicit, and the instructions include silent loading. This is a confirmed local secret persistence pattern.

medium_findings.title: Autopilot Mode Can Plan and Spawn Work Without Per-Task Approval
medium_findings.description: The playbook supports an autopilot mode that can monitor, plan, create tasks, spawn workers, review, merge, and loop. The skill asks the user to choose the mode, but the resulting autonomy increases operational risk.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The autonomy model is clearly documented and user selectable. It is not malicious by itself, but it can amplify mistakes or misuse after credentials are configured.

medium_findings.title: Network API Surface Includes Team, Role, Task, Worker, and Event Operations
medium_findings.description: The skill documents many Agentix API calls for team state, roles, tasks, workers, events, and API key creation. These calls are expected for the product, but broad API authority increases abuse impact.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network endpoints are explicit and cover many privileged workflow operations. The calls are not hidden, but they form a broad control surface.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Agentix Documentation and Service URLs
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to the declared Agentix service, localhost examples, self-hosted examples, and public documentation. These are expected for an API integration and are not suspicious alone.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs match the skill purpose and documented setup flow. They matter as part of the broader network risk but are not independently malicious.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Matches Are Documentation Terms
low_findings.description: The static weak cryptography findings appear to match words such as API reference, mode text, or endpoint examples rather than cryptographic implementation. No evidence found of hashing or encryption code in SKILL.md.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The skill file is Markdown documentation and API instructions, not executable cryptographic code. The flagged lines do not show weak algorithm use.

low_findings.title: Backtick Static Matches Are Markdown Formatting
low_findings.description: Most Ruby or shell backtick execution findings are false positives caused by Markdown inline code and fenced examples. The risk comes from instructed API workflows, not Ruby backtick execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: SKILL.md contains Markdown code formatting and endpoint examples, not Ruby source code. The scanner correctly noticed command-like text but mislabeled the execution mechanism.

dangerous_patterns.title: Secrets Sent to External API
dangerous_patterns.description: User-provided API keys and tokens are included in Agentix API request bodies for team and git configuration. This requires informed user consent and strong vendor trust.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples show credential values being passed to the API. The surrounding text confirms the agent should ask for those secrets and avoid displaying them.

dangerous_patterns.title: Silent Loading of Local Credentials
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill instructs agents to load existing credentials silently from a hidden file. Silent reuse can surprise users and may bypass explicit confirmation in later sessions.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The instruction to load values silently is explicit. This is useful for continuity but risky for a marketplace skill handling secrets.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Worker Spawn Endpoints
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes endpoints to start and resume remote workers for tasks. This can execute user work outside the local environment and should be restricted to trusted deployments.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The worker endpoints are directly documented and match the skill description. The concern is execution boundary risk, not hidden behavior.

content.value_statement: Managing many AI workers can become hard to coordinate across roles, tasks, and progress states. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a structured Agentix workflow for team setup, task assignment, and worker monitoring.
actual_capabilities: Guides setup for Agentix SaaS or a self-hosted Agentix instance.
Creates and updates teams, roles, tasks, and playbooks through Agentix API endpoints.
Starts or resumes Agentix workers for ready tasks.
Monitors workers, task status, and event activity feeds.
Stores team credentials for reuse across sessions.
limitations: Requires access to Agentix SaaS or a compatible self-hosted Agentix API.
Handles sensitive API keys and tokens, so it should only be used with trusted accounts.
Remote workers depend on the Agentix platform and configured model provider keys.
Autopilot workflows require careful review before enabling on important projects.

use_cases.title: Coordinate Engineering Work
use_cases.description: Create specialized roles, split work into tasks, start workers, and monitor progress from one workflow.

use_cases.title: Operate a Self-Hosted Agentix Team
use_cases.description: Connect an assistant to a private Agentix instance and reuse a default team configuration.

use_cases.title: Run Supervised AI Task Pipelines
use_cases.description: Track task queues and worker activity while keeping approval control over new work.

prompt_templates.title: Set Up My Agentix Team
prompt_templates.prompt: Set up Agentix for my project. Ask for only the details you need, explain each step, and use supervised mode by default.

prompt_templates.title: Create Roles and Tasks
prompt_templates.prompt: Create Agentix roles for this project and turn my goal into ready tasks. Tell me which roles you create and why.

prompt_templates.title: Monitor Current Work
prompt_templates.prompt: Check my Agentix team status, list active workers, summarize recent events, and recommend the next supervised action.

prompt_templates.title: Run an Autopilot Cycle
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the playbook, confirm autopilot mode, plan the next work batch, spawn appropriate workers, and report progress without exposing secrets.

output_examples.input: Set up a new team for a backend API project.
output: Requests the user name and email for registration.
Creates or reuses an Agentix team.
Summarizes the configured team and next steps.

output_examples.input: Check what my workers are doing.
output: Lists running workers and related tasks.
Keeps API keys and tokens out of chat output.

output_examples.input: Create roles for a web app launch.
output: Defines focused roles such as frontend engineer and reviewer.
Explains why each role is useful.
Waits for approval before spawning workers in supervised mode.

best_practices: Use supervised mode until the team, credentials, and worker behavior are trusted.
Store only the minimum credentials required for the current Agentix team.
Review tasks and playbook policies before enabling worker execution.
anti_patterns: Do not paste secrets into public chat logs or issue trackers.
Do not enable autopilot for repositories or accounts you cannot risk changing.
Do not reuse broad GitHub tokens when a narrower token can complete the work.

faq.question: What does this skill connect to?
faq.answer: It connects to Agentix SaaS by default or to a self-hosted Agentix API when configured.

faq.question: Does it need API keys?
faq.answer: Yes. SaaS setup uses an Agentix API key, and workers may need an Anthropic API key.

faq.question: Can it use GitHub?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill can configure a repository URL and GitHub token for worker tasks.

faq.question: Is it safe for sensitive projects?
faq.answer: Use caution. The skill handles secrets and remote workers, so it should be used only with trusted Agentix deployments.

faq.question: Can it run without Agentix SaaS?
faq.answer: Yes. It supports self-hosted Agentix instances through the AGENTIX_API_URL setting.

faq.question: What is the recommended operating mode?
faq.answer: Supervised mode is recommended first because it keeps user approval in the workflow.
